sentences.text: Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we arrived in Auschwitz, I-- I didn't know what that putrid smell is all about.
And-- and while we didn't know where we were, what type of place it was, it was just nauseating.
But I just dismissed it from my mind.
But once I learned that we were in a death camp, and once I was assigned to work just behind one of the gas chambers and crematorium, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew very well what this was all about.

And-- and when my-- when I associated these columns of people who went in to gas chamber and crematorium number four, which is the one that was close by-- 20, 25 feet away from me-- I-- I knew what that smell was all about.
And it affected me tremendously.
It-- it made me terribly nauseous, and I-- I was physically affected by it.
I knew that I won't be able to keep up with-- keep up with this.
I must get used to it, or I'll perish.
And then one day soon, I completely lost my sense of smell in Auschwitz.
For 47 years, I couldn't smell anything.
My sense of taste remained quite keen.
But the doctor thought that it was probably psychological.
I don't think they really knew for sure.
I went through all sorts of tests, but they found nothing wrong.
It did not affect my cooking.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's amazing to me how the human body is able to adjust to-- to horror.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If we really want to, we-- I think-- I think it's possible for us to go through just about anything.
If-- if-- if we can go through Auschwitz and come out half sane, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I think we can go through just about anything.
Yes.
Well, that-- that number remain my number throughout my concentration camp ordeal.
Strips of cloth were given to us.
We had to sew it on, on one side, in front and on the back of our single garment that we owned.
Yes.
Throughout my concentration camp period.
I'm not 100% certain if that number really was the same number.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I just presume that it was.
I want to be sure to-- to-- to mention this, because it could have been that they gave me another number in the other camps.
I just don't remember it.
Somehow, this number is so clear in my mind.
I never forgot it.
And it was the first camp.
I went through six additional camps, and I can't remember any of the other numbers, which leads me to believe that this was my number throughout.
Yes.
We were given some sort of job in Bergen-Belsen.
I remember it was-- the area was hilly, and we handed bricks to each other-- from one to another.
And I'm not even sure if it was bricks, because that's exactly what we did in the ghetto Beregszasz.
They-- they didn't want us to-- to be idle.
Actually, I wasn't even involved.
I just watched the grown-ups, the men and the boys, to hand bricks to each other.
Well, we did-- they used that same process with us in Bergen-Belsen.
We handed something to each other.
I just remember that it was something, but I'm not 100% sure what it really was.
But we-- it was heavy.
It could have been rocks.
And it was sort of a futile type of process, because there was nothing that they were building with this, that I could tell.
And this was a much smaller camp than Auschwitz, as I could tell.
So that-- that was it.
I was in Bergen-Belsen for a short time, and from there I was shipped out in the middle of the night.
And we arrived in the industrial city of Braunschweig.
And there we were housed in the-- in the barn of the horses of the SS.
But there were rings along the side wall-- along one side of the wall of this barn-- long, long barn.
And it was also located in the midst of a residential area.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where we were housed.
The horses were taken out and tied to these rings outside, and we slept on the concrete floor.
About 600 to 800 of us performed slave labor in Braunschweig.
We slept on the floor, which had some damp and moldy straw-- well, on it.
And in the mornings we were counted off, and taken to our job site.
In the-- in this barn, they provided for us by creating-- by slashing a hole through the cement floor, which became our latrine.
In one corner of the barn was-- in a triangle shape-- was the housing of the SS kommandant-- a female kommandant.
And I believe that her name was Hermina Braunsteiner.
A woman SS.
And she had sort of a dirty blonde hair, and a very bad [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] permanent.
It was fuzzy hair.
And she wore a-- a black cape, and had always a whip in her hand.
And she was vicious.
And she had a dog.
And at the slightest provocation, that dog would just climb up on us and-- and mar us, and just tear us to pieces.
There were-- I believe there were two guards.
I-- I only remember two, who were placed in front of the barn.
Now, remember, this was not the setting of Auschwitz.
It was a city, which was terribly-- suffered terribly from-- from bombardments.
And our job was to open up the major streets of bombarded debris, so the German artillery and vehicles could pass through.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once in a while, on our way to work, we-- we would encounter some kind civilians.

I remember one time, a little old lady-- her hair was up in a bun here, and sort of graying, and she had this huge French-- French bread under-- tucked under her arm-- and her hair-- head was sort of stooped.
And she looked this way and that way, and she was just waiting for the right time for her to throw us that bread.
And she did.
And it felt so good that there was-- there are some human beings out there who care about us, and who risked their life-- lives for us.
Something funny happened on our job once, which I'd like to share.
One time, we-- we were lined up in a different sort of way on this very wide boulevard.
And we were divided into small groups of four, or five, or six of us.
I think it was five or six.
Anyway, and so the-- the couple of guards just went back and forth in a long area.
So it gave us a little chance to, sort of, see what else is there.
After all, this-- where we were working, that used to be a house at one time, and maybe there is-- is there something.
And one time-- actually, when we least expected it-- someone from my small group just disappeared.
And that was dangerous for us, because we could be accused of-- of helping her escape.
And maybe we all could get shot as a result, because lives were cheap.
But what happened was that we could not hear her.
Well, we were using shovels, and from the clattering of noise, we could not hear as she fell through a hole into the basement of a bombed-out building.
And we looked around, and we-- you know, we couldn't see her anywhere.
And we became very concerned.
And suddenly there was a voice coming up from way down under.
No pun intended for Australia, sorry.

And this voice said that 'I am-- I have fallen through a hole into the basement.' And so when we could see that the coast was fairly clear, we yelled down, 'Look around for some food.' And so she did.
And when the right time came-- came again, we whistled down and let her know that we were ready to pull her up.
And when we did, her large man's pockets were bulging with black round things.
And indeed, they were potatoes, baked and burned from the fire of the bomb.
So we feasted on baked, burned potatoes while on that job, and I can tell you, they were delicious.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I was not permitted to go down there.
I was really everybody's little sister.
But my job was to look out for the guards, and whistle, and that sort of thing.
I used to whistle pretty well.
Not anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
There were many allied planes that visited Braunschweig while-- while we were there.
We-- so far as we could see, everything was already flat.
Rubble-- reduced to rubble.
How-- what more is there to bomb?
But they were still in the distance, bombing there.
But I remember one time, a bomber flew so low that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- we all flattened on the ground.
And they-- they started dropping bombs.
And well, I flattened on the ground, but some of my fellow inmates received shrapnel, and some died.
Several died, actually, at this bombing.
I guess they didn't know that-- who we were.
They didn't recognize us.
And so-- and the dead was just left there.
We would have to bring the food with us in a large canister.
And the food was pretty much the same throughout-- throughout my camp life.
And I really think it was so terrible that it can hardly be called food.
We would receive a dark liquid in the morning that you can call tea or coffee.
It was neither.
It was brewed from dry leaves that inmates who were assigned to that kommando picked up in the outer perimeter of the camp.
A friend of mine was on that-- in that kommando.
That's when I learned it was not tea.
It was bitter.
There was no sugar in it, of course.
And in Auschwitz, yet, my mother taught me to eat, drink every drop of it, because at least this may have been boiled, and this may be safe fluid, and there isn't much else.
We did receive a ration of bread each morning, until much later, when they skipped days here and there, or when we would travel.
We would travel for two, three days without any provisions, several times.
And this took a tremendous toll on the human body, especially in our weakened and emaciated condition.
Of course, in the beginning, we were not emaciated.
But as-- soon, we-- we lost weight.
And-- and it didn't take very long before we reached that stage when we could-- I could really feel my rib bones, and my hip bones.
I really-- and here today, I'm-- I wish I could lose about 50 pounds.
The bread was approximately-- maybe two slices of bread for the day.
We-- we received some soup in the evening.
Sometimes at lunch, sometimes in the evening.
And in the evening, also another ration of-- of that brown liquid that-- that we drank.
What protein?
Oh, it had-- it had potatoes-- a half a potato, cooked or uncooked, in it.
In fact, this helps me to remember another incident in Auschwitz.
And if you don't mind, I'd like to record that.
In Auschwitz, a few days after we-- we were there-- only a few days-- it must have been the first three days, because we were not working yet.
My mother, who had artificial upper teeth that came in-- only the upper teeth.
Well, she bit into the potato in her soup, and it was not cooked.
She bit into it, and she cracked her upper teeth.
And she was devastated after that.
She could no longer chew her uncooked potato.
And sometimes it was a beet.
The beet was softer.
And I helped her chew her potato, or beet.
I don't remember the beet, actually.
I remember the potato.
But I-- so the beet must have been much more-- much softer, come to think of it.
And-- and I felt that this is better than not having it at all.
After-- mother didn't have any upper teeth until-- ever, after that.
To-- to get back to Braunschweig again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this hole in the ground that we used, that we called latrine, was-- how shall I describe it?
It was only about this wide, and it had crude wood, like branches, up, and it connected on top.
It was built especially for us, and-- so that we could hold on to it only at either end.
It was too tall to hold onto.
It was only to keep those bars together.
But I need to draw you a picture of it.
We-- there was a board on which we sat, and that was our latrine.
Now, we were not permitted to go to the latrine at night, not in Braunschweig.
And so this young girl of about 16 years of age, suffering from acute dysentery, rushed to the latrine.
She just couldn't hold herself back anymore.
And on the way, the lagerkommandant saw her through her window, and came out with her whip and whipped her to the ground.
And-- but the girl insisted on going, and she-- she went to the latrine.
But she was too weak to hold herself up, and she just drowned.
She fell in and drowned.
And they left her there for three days for us to see what will happen to us if we go to the latrine at night.
And keep her from resting.
The sight will never, ever leave my memory.
That's Braunschweig.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had long days, and I think this sort of summarizes the events in Braunschweig.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there, we were sent to-- I was sent to Hanover, in an area of Hanover that was called Linden-Limmer.
L-I-N-D-E-N capital L-I-M-M-E-R.
Linden-Limmer.
There was a huge factory called the Continental Gummi Werke.
Continental-- Gummi, G-U-M-M-I-- Werke-- W-E-R-K-E, which means the Continental Rubber Factory.
I performed slave labor at the Continental Gummi Werke, at the age of 15.
My job was to-- to-- well, we worked on an assembly line there.
And we worked on gas masks.
Hitler wanted all his subjects to have a gas mask, fearing poison gas attacks by the allies-- which, however, never materialized.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a result of that, he decided to save a few of us and put us into the-- his factories throughout Germany.
I-- now, there was another reason, of course, and for different purposes.
The men of Germany were in the service, and the factories needed to be run.
And so we were distributed into all these factories in different places, doing different things.
So I will describe to you what I did in-- in Hanover and so forth, in the-- in the-- in the future camps.
On this assembly-- this assembly line kept moving, and each of us to perform a different function on a gas mask.
And if we could not finish our function in-- of that gas mask, we were to take it and just drop it by our side.
Now-- and all of us had found ourselves in a situation that we could not finish it.
They could adjust that belt different-- at different speed, and so sometimes it went so fast that one-- it was impossible for us to even touch it.
So sometimes we had to just drop it.
And of course, this was a way that they weeded us out.
And those people just disappeared.
Our lives were cheap.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] As a slave laborer, we lived on the factory grounds, surrounded by electric wire fences.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Across from the electric wire fences, away from the-- the factory and our barrack, we could see just charming little summer homes.
Flowers, very neatly trimmed.
And we saw, sometimes, children, and civilians, and we thought that they were-- they belonged to the SS, that that's where they lived.
Only to find out later that they were the civilians.
These were charming little summer homes.
They moved out of the city and moved into their summer homes.
The Continental Gummi-- Gummi Werke is, if not largest, it-- it could be the second largest factory in all of Europe today.
It was founded by Jews.
And through research, I found out it was found by Jews way back in the middle of the 19th century, in the-- around 1850.
And maybe it was even earlier.
I could give it to you exactly later.
And on its board of directors, there were a number of Jews, and non-Jews as well.
And as we go year by year from the time that Hitler came to power, you could see that the board of directors-- the Jews were removed from the board of directors.
Until 1939, there was only one token Jew on the board of directors of the Continental Gummi Werke, and this person apparently was a diplomat from Sweden.
After 1939, he, too, was no longer there.
And the factory was totally taken over by-- by the SS and by the government.
Yes, indeed.
There were civilians who worked there.
In fact, they were in the back.
They were not together with us.
We were-- we were not permitted to come in contact with-- with them.
But-- but they complained at one time, when we were beaten up for not doing our job.
We used to be beaten up right there, on the assembly line.

And, of course, you know, when we have our head-- hands up trying to protect us from-- from the whip-- from the whip, we can't possibly do anything on-- on the moving-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot what it's called-- conveyor belt.
So they-- they complained that they don't like to watch these emaciated people get beaten up.
So what did the SS do?
They decided to invent a new way.
So from there on, we weren't beaten on the job anymore.
Instead, they just-- number so-and-so, number so-and-so, and so-and-so, had a-- a long list by the end of the day who-- who did not do her share of-- of work on the assembly line.
So we would-- after 12 hours working on these-- on these gas masks, we'd get back to our barracks and we would dream about it at night.
They were like monsters in our dreams.
They would-- they would appear as monsters.
They-- they were horrible.
So that wasn't enough.
Our numbers were called up.
And-- and then we had to take the-- the whip.
Get what came to us.
What came to us, they said.
That we deserved punishment.
How did that affect you?
Were you one of those?
Oh-- Oh, I think all of us, all of us received it.
I only remember the 25 or more.
And nothing else-- nothing else counts after that.
I mean, anything below 20, even, wasn't considered so-- even though your life maybe may depend on it.
I just-- I remember the major ones.
We all-- we all had lots of whips.
Because, you see, in the camps, they told us-- somehow they let us know it's breakfast time.
Breakfast.
Zahlappell time.
And there was a small exit from the barrack.
And we would all try to rush out, because they were beating us.
And somebody was beating us on the other side, and somebody was beating us from this side.
So very often there was cuts, and slash-- I don't know.
I got it many times-- but-- from these things.
